speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 4 with Survivor Howard Chandler. Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I figure to myself, why don't I look around the other barracks? Maybe I could find my father or my brother. I knew there were all different people arriving from different camps there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had nothing to lose. So every day, after-- of course, after I got my Russian so I'd understand, I used to go scour the different barracks. And after a few days, I found my brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- he was sent to a place called Buna, or Monowitz, which was also part of the Auschwitz complex. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they evacuated him in the same columns as we were going, except maybe he was a day ahead of us, or something like this. And I found him there. I went back to my barrack. And I appealed to my Blockalteste, which was this fellow, Gustav, the Russian prisoner of war. I could speak Russian then because in Auschwitz, I was in a barrack with Russian prisoners of war. And I picked up Russian quite easily.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I asked him for-- that my brother should come and be with us-- with, with me in the same barrack. He says, you know, how do you expect him to come here? We're overcrowded as it is.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I insisted and persisted. He says, well, after a few people die, maybe there'll be room. And if they don't assign us more people, maybe we'll take him. And then after a while, that's exactly what happened. I brought my brother. And he stayed with me in the same barrack. And I think-- Elie Wiesel was in same barrack. Of course then, he was just [LAUGHS] another body there. And we stayed there until April, 1945, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when the camp was starting to be evacuated. We heard rumors that there is an Underground in the camp, that there are political prisoners there. And maybe we'll be [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] freed from there. We didn't know what to make of it. You always heard rumors, but how true they were, we didn't know. The fact is that they started to evacuate the camp. First of all, they took the Jewish prisoners out. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then they resisted from going. They burned the, the-- what do you call the [NON-ENGLISH], the, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the offices where they kept the names-- the index. So nobody knew who, who was who.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they used to come around-- the SS used to come around the camp and pick out those who were Jews. Then one SS man got killed. Somebody took a spade or a shovel and killed him. So they killed a prisoner. And they killed all these people who were bystanders there that were watching. It didn't do anything to help the German.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they used to come with bigger search parties and pick out prisoners for evacuation. They used to evacuate the people in the morning. And the same train used to come back in the afternoon for more prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they mean by evacuation?

speaker: interviewee
text: They would assemble a certain amount of prisoners, fill out the train, and the train used to leave. They used to shoot them. I don't know. They took them to some valley or to some unknown place. They used to shoot them, bring back the train, and more people would be taken out. And you could see the same train coming out then-- coming back empty. So people didn't want to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we figured, you can resist only so much. So I said to my brother and to my friends, you know what? We can hold out tomorrow, maybe the day after. But eventually, they are going to evacuate the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our best plan would be-- is to go with the Russian prisoners of war. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were-- they were trustworthy people. You could talk to them. You could confide in them. And we figure our best chance to run away is with them. They are trained people. They know what to do with weapons if they get hold of them. And this is what happened. They started to evacuate the Russian prisoners. And my brother and I, my friends, we joined them. They marched us down to Weimar, which is about eight kilometers. And they put us in an open freight train. I don't know, maybe 80 or 90 or a hundred people to a-- to a carload, to a wagon. They gave us two days of rations. We were allowed to take a blanket. And off the train went. And it was April, where we had cool days. We had warm days. We had rainy days. No roof-- we got wet. There's no chance to getting dry. The two days of rations disappeared pretty quickly. And we traveled for about four weeks like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Four weeks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four weeks. We were being shuffled there and back, depending on which-- where the front was. We stopped in places. We stayed one or two days. The hunger was so great. The people didn't know what to eat. That is the only place I, I, I-- when I witnessed cannibalism taking place. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this was amongst the Russian prisoners of war. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I knew the officers. I knew who-- which one the officer is already. I went to complain-- those who were doing the, the cutting up of a-- of a person there. He was barely, barely dead. I figured they'll come and kill him, you know. But the officers came. They admonished him. They gave him a big tongue lashing, you know. They called him all kinds of names. And it stopped. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] But the Russian prisoners of war, because they were soldiers, they knew what to do with the-- with the guards. Instead of one person or two persons jumping off a train after it stopped, they would jump off en masse. So they couldn't shoot everybody. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when we jumped off the train, we could eat the grass. We could eat the fresh leaves coming on the trees. It was April, you know. Anything that moved there, we caught and ate. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One day, they gave us some food to eat, which was cabbage. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was preserved in salt. And what you ate is half, half salt and half cabbage. And I could realize it is going to kill us right away. And I told my friends to stop eating it. Wait until the next day. We would add water to it, maybe dilute it. And we start to fight over it. And while we were boiling it, my friend got burned because somebody tried to grab it away from him. Anyway, when this journey ended-- which I have some pictures here-- from about 3,000 to 4,000 prisoners, there were about 500 prisoners left alive-- when we were liberated about-- exactly on the 8th of May, 1945. We wound up in Terezin or Theresienstadt in Czechoslovakia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of all the days in the prison, this journey was the worst.

speaker: interviewer
text: Four weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Four weeks on two days of, of rations.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go after you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we were liberated, we, we again were segregated from the general population in Terezin. There were Kaserne, you know. Theresienstadt Ghetto or, or Camp was made up of a former army camp. There were barracks. It was a garrison town. And there were multi-story buildings for prisoners, you know. There was the Hamburg Kaserne and the Hanover Kaserne.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they used to take youth out after liberation, after the Russians came and liberated us. There was a Russian doctor. I think he was Jewish. I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: He picked up all the young people. And they put us in a-- in a separate house, where we would-- I, I believe we were getting extra attention there. They would assign extra nurses, maybe give us better-- more food, which was a killer right away. We used to overeat. We used to get food we weren't used to eating. The boys used to get diarrhea. And the whole building was like a toilet. And they used to die from eating. And you had to be quick about it and recognize that you shouldn't eat. My brother, he was-- I didn't know one day he was dead, one day he was alive. On the train there, I, I got beaten up three times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the trains used to stop. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We would look for food. And we traveled together with the German military trains. When we stopped on a station where you couldn't go no more-- there was our train. And there were maybe two or three military trains. And they had their soldiers on it. And they had their anti-aircraft guns on it. The airplanes used to come and strafe us. They used to shoot at them. We used to get strafed and killed. I wasn't afraid of getting killed, or that I cared of getting killed or not. My mind-- on my mind and my friend's mind was getting some food. So we used to watch where the German military trains were-- where the kitchens were, and where the supplies wagons were. And I spied a supply wagon on the German train, which was parked right-- the next-- the next straightaway line to us. I told my friends, I says, OK. I was more or less the organizer there. I'm going to jump on. But you have-- you know what to do. When I jump down, you got to protect me, so the other prisoners don't jump on me, trying to take it away. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was one German-- every time there was an air raid, the German soldiers used to run away and hide themselves. There was one German-- I don't know whether it was his job or he did it on his own-- he used to hide behind another wagon, with his pistol drawn. And every time he saw somebody jump on the supply wagon, he used to pick off and shoot them. So there was about three or four bodies lying on the bottom there. That didn't distract me at all. I used to watch him. And I jumped on the wagon. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got some rutabagas, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like turnips. I got some bones. And there was some meat on the bones. And I got some other food there. I stuffed them in my pants. I watched my exit. And I watched him-- every time he took a shot, I jumped. He took a shot at somebody, and I jumped at the same time. Anyway, my friends weren't there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The other prisoners jumped me, tried to take  the food away. I tried to save it. And I got beaten up. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When we were in Czechoslovakia, the Czech people came out. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] A few days later, after this, we were so cold, I, I made a little fire on my wagon in our corner. I, I took the, the, the steel plates that the-- that the railway lines sit on, and I put them on the wagon on the wood. And I made a little fire [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] once, or twice, or three times. And one day, when the train started to move-- I didn't realize it that the wood underneath these iron plates burned out, and there was a hole underneath it. And when the train-- they started shifting the train, the coals fell through. They stopped the train. They came to our wagon. The Obersturmfuhrer came there and wanted to know who committed the sabotage. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And nobody said anything. And they insisted and insisted. And I figured, somebody's going to tell them. It's going to be worse. So sheepishly, I went over. I says, I did. He took me down. He got hold of a big pole. It must have been a new branch from a tree, still with the-- with the nuts sticking out. And he started beating the hell out of me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I started to run away. He ran after me. The train was moving very slowly. I ran under the train. I ran out the other side.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I ran with the train. And the prisoners put their hands down, picked me up. And I was half-dead already-- a few wagons past my wagon with my friends. And then that-- they closed the train up again. And, and we left. So that's the second time I got beaten up. The third time I got beaten up was in Czechoslovakia. When the Czech people came, they saw us. And from the bridges and from the high place, started throwing cakes at us, and bread. I was indifferent already. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is when I saw these people come and take these, these pictures there. The Czech-- the Czech flags were flying. We didn't know where we were. The guards were still there. When these partisans came, they took the guards away. And they finished them off. I think they did. We didn't see it, but I heard shots. And the train went into Terezin. We stayed there for about a week or so. And the Russian Army came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in-- while we were in Terezin, we were segregated, put in a-- in a youth house. And the Red Cross came. And they wanted to help us. And the commandant-- his name was Kuzmin, Russian commandant at Theresienstadt. He liberated the place. He was concerned about us, where we're going. We, we wanted to go to Palestine. We didn't want to go anywhere else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I believe the French Red Cross came. We refused to go to France. And then the Swiss Red Cross came. And we refused to go there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eventually, he made a deal with the British Red Cross that we, we would be allowed to England, provided we will be given certificates to go to Palestine after we will be rehabilitated. And that's what it was. We went to England in August 1948. And we, we were taken to Prague. We waited for the RAF-- I don't what it was. I think the Lancaster bombers, they came. They loaded us up, and they took us to England. We landed in Carlisle, from where we were taken to a place called Windermere. They had an an industrial hostel there-- where it was occupied during the war by aircraft workers. They were making aircraft there for the RAF, I guess for the British. And these people were no, no, no longer needed. The war was over. And we occupied this hostel. There was about 300 of us there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Around the same ages?

speaker: interviewee
text: Around the same ages. And there we formed groups. And we formed new friendships. And they sent us to different places. They would rent a house someplace in the-- in the-- in the country, or wherever in England-- in Liverpool and Manchester and Luton and Bedford and Bradford. And groups would go. And they would assign teachers or [NON-ENGLISH]. And we pick, say, a, a trade to learn and attend school. We were given the chances. And we were given medical attention. We were given clothes, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: End of 1947, at the end of '47, just before Christmastime, I came to Canada, under a scheme from the Jewish Congress. Canada allowed a number of Jewish orphans. You had to be an orphan to come. My brother couldn't come because he was over 18. I was under 18. I just made it. And I came to Canada with the understanding that if I like it here, I would be able to bring him over here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how you met your, your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I met my wife here at a dance at the YMHA.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how specifically you met your wife at the dance?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I think she was trying to put her shoes on, and she couldn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --manage. I tried to help her, you know. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you married when?

speaker: interviewee
text: We married in November, 1951.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Elsa, E-L-S-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Biller, B-I-L-L-E-R. And she comes from Lwow or Lemberg in Galicia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long have you been married for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've been married since 1951. So so I guess 44 years?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. And how many children?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're four children and four grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wonderful. On that note, I would like to take this opportunity to sincerely thank you for sharing a very critical and important part of your life with the rest of us. And thank you for making us much more aware and teaching us. Thank you again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. I hope people do learn from it. It's an important lesson to learn, that things like this are never repeated again under any circumstances. And fortunately, so far, it hasn't happened. And I'm, I'm, I'm saddened by the situation throughout the world-- what is going on now. And that people-- I know it happened over 50 years ago. History, it's-- for history, it's, it's very, very recent. And sometimes I wonder whether you can depend on the-- on the present leadership or any future leadership to prevent things like this. So I hope a interview like this, more younger people will learn and take note of it to prevent, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you again.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my paternal grandparents-- Majer and Chaja Wajchendler, probably taken in the city or the town of Szydlowiec in the province of Kielce in Poland, sometimes in the early 1900s. That's a picture of my maternal grandmother, Chaja Sztarkman, and my mother as a girl of approximately 10-- 10 years old, that is, taken in the city of Radom, Poland. And this is a picture of my parents-- Pearl Blima Sztarkman and my father Leibka Wajchendler. I would imagine this was taken just prior or just after their wedding. I don't know exactly when they got married. It must have been in the late '20s. That, too, is a picture of my mother and father. I would imagine it was taken also in the-- sometimes in the '20s, in the city of Radom, I would imagine, or Wierzbnik. This is a picture of my extended family, who mostly resided in the city of Radom. And watching this picture, I could see one, two, three, persons out of this family who survived the Holocaust, the youngest amongst whom is my cousin, Henik Wajsglas, who after the war, became the chief medical officer of Croatia. He went to Yugoslavia to study medicine. And he stayed there after the war. And the rest are my uncles and my aunts. My, my aunt in the middle, she survived and lived in England. And that's my Uncle Uszer. And that's my cousin, Balshe. And that's my Uncle Pinche and his wife Gutche, [NON-ENGLISH], and two other cousins. This is a picture of the train after we left Buchenwald in the-- in April of 1945, on the way to Theresienstadt in Czechoslovakia. This picture was taken by the partisans just prior to the liberation. And me-- I'm on this wagon, somewhere in this corner. And my other friends are quite recognizable. Some of them survived, and some did not because they couldn't pull through the, the, the, the trauma, or whatever it is. They were undernourished. And some of them died. And most of them, thank goodness, we are alive and living in different countries now. I could identify them by name and where they live. Benik Bulfrowicz, he lives in Los Angeles someplace. Mundek Hersztajn, my best friend-- he died. He couldn't survive. He died some-- for medical-- for medical reasons. Burgierman, he lives in England. Alterman-- Icek Alterman, he lives in England. I forget his name. I forget his name. Lajbel-- I forget his other name. And these are-- these are my friends with, with whom I traveled from-- out of Buchenwald to Theresienstadt. You will notice that the wagon is almost full. Some of us died on the way, in contrast to the other wagons, where most of the people died. This is another snapshot taken of the same transport. And they are mostly Russian prisoners of war. They, they, they have been badly decimated. As you can see, the rest of the wagon-- the, the people who are on this wagon are not alive anymore. And this is how big a toll this journey of the four weeks between April and May took. This is-- this was taken most likely early in May 1945, just prior to the liberation. And this is another picture of the same transport, of course. And they are mostly Russian prisoners of war. And this is a view of the inside of the-- of the wagon. The wagons were with no roof. And we traveled between April and May. And you can see the blankets being hang out on the corner, which we-- which we were allowed to take after we left Buchenwald, constantly wet from the rains. And here you see the condition of the prisoners. Some are dead. Some are almost dead. And some are just oblivious to what is going on around them. Although liberation was quite near-- and it was noticeable amongst those who were still healthy, so to speak, who were still observant. They could observe what's taking place. I was like, like these people were, quite oblivious to what is taking place around me because I got beaten up quite badly on three occasions in a short span of time. And this is what happens to you. This is a picture of a Czech partisan or, or guerrilla fighter, who took these snaps just prior to the liberation. And he came to Theresienstadt where we were unloaded and housed. And he found us. And he give us these pictures that he took. This is a picture of myself on the left and my older brother, Harry, on the right, which was taken in July-- that is May, June, July-- two months after liberation in Terezin or Theresienstadt in Czechoslovakia. This is a picture of my brother, Harry, taken in August of 1945 in Theresienstadt. That's three months after liberation. This is a picture of me in October, 1945 taken in Windermere, with Lake Windermere, I believe, in the background. This is a wedding picture of me and my wife, Elsa, taken November 4, 1951, where we got married-- in the city of Toronto. It's my pleasure to introduce to my immediate family. Besides me is my lovely wife, Elsa. And my children, in order of seniority-- behind me in the glasses with the lovely face on, that's Hedi [LAUGHTER] and her husband, [? Denny. ?] I introduce the grandchildren later on. There is Dr. Bonnie Chandler-- 'Dr.,' I emphasize that. That's an achievement-- and her son Ariel. Her husband, or dad, Hubert, is unable to be with us, unfortunately. Next is Joyce and her husband--

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Jeff. ?] [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? --Jeff ?] Pearlston. And her-- and their son, Aaron, is right here with us. Give us a smile, Aaron. Good boy. [LAUGHTER] And then is my son, Mark, Mark Chandler. His wife is unfortunately not able to be with us. Her name is Dr. Iris [? Kibiti. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And my grandchildren, four boys, beautiful boys-- David, who is six years old, right, David? Seven?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven? Going on seven?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm, I'm seven and a half.

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven and a half-- he corrected me. That's Aaron Pearlston, my-- Joyce's son.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he's six.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he is six. Then there's Daren. How old are you, Daren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three years old--

speaker: interviewee
text: Three and a half.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and growing strong.

speaker: interviewee
text: And David-- I'd be this old--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then there's Ariel-- Ariel Hubert Chandler.

speaker: interviewee
text: How old are you, honey?

speaker: interviewee
text: How old are you, Ari? Two years old and going strong. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would any one else like to make a comment? Elsa, why don't you start and warm them up? [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, here's your chance to say a few words. You have an input into the Spielberg-- what do you call it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Survivors of the Shoah Foundation.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- Shoah of the Survivors Foundation. They are making tapes of testimonies from the survivors. And naturally, since you're being included, a few words would be quite appropriate.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hedi should start. She's the oldest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good idea, huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: I get too emotional. Well, we certainly appreciate this opportunity, for my parents especially, to be able to have their histories down on tape for a long time, I hope, and always keep proof that there was a Holocaust. And it did destroy many lives. And, we're very proud of our parents. It's because of them that we are what we are today, because of their experiences. And over the years, we experience what they experienced in, in a secondhand way, but nonetheless, with great affect. And we're terribly proud of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sit like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyone-- anybody else want to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm kind of at a loss for words. But I'm very proud of both my parents. They worked very hard at bringing us all up in a mensch-like way. Two years ago, I went back to Poland with my father and a friend of his and his three sons. And he showed us his house that we-- he grew up in. It was very moving, touching, I guess-- a difficult experience for my father to go back. It wasn't the first time he was back. I know that he went back with Hedi.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went.

speaker: interviewee
text: With Bonnie. And I know that he spends a lot of time going to high schools. He's become friends with some history teachers that-- he goes up to Collingwood, I think, every year to speak about the Holocaust. And it means a lot. I went with him once to a Catholic high school where he spoke to high school students. And the impression I got from the students was that they think the Holocaust occurred, you know, thousands of years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- when they see a person that's there that, that takes the time, like my father, to explain it to them that it wasn't really that long ago. It, it means a lot to them. And I know it means a lot for my father to do that. Bonnie?

speaker: interviewer
text: Bonnie?
